question_text: A sealed syringe contains air at atmospheric pressure (101 kPa). When the plunger is pushed in to reduce the volume to one-third of its original size, what is the new pressure inside the syringe, assuming the temperature stays constant?
explanation: Using Boyle's Law (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂), when volume decreases to one-third, pressure must increase three times. 101 kPa × 3 = 303 kPa. This shows the inverse relationship between pressure and volume for gases at constant temperature.
